6: hos en morphe theou hyparchon ouch harpagmon hegesato to einai isa theo (hos en morphē theou hyparchōn ouch harpagmon hēgēsato to einai isa theō): the most debated sentence in Philippians. Morphe theou (form/mode of God) is not mere appearance (doxa/eidos) but the actual mode of existence — the divine being's characteristic reality. Harpagmon (HARPAGMOS): the crux. Older interpretation: 'did not regard equality with God as something to be grasped (and held onto)' — the pre-existent Son relinquished divine prerogatives. Newer consensus (N.T. Wright, R. Hoover): 'did not regard equality with God as something to exploit for his own advantage' — already possessing it, he did not use it selfishly. The second reading requires no ontological descent at incarnation — the Son freely chose not to exploit his divine position. Either way, the kenosis (self-emptying, v. 7) is voluntary and constitutes the pattern for Christian ethics (v. 5).
9: dio kai ho theos auton hyperypsosen kai echarisato auto to onoma to hyper pan onoma (dio kai ho theos auton hyperypsōsen kai echarisato autō to onoma to hyper pan onoma): 'Therefore God has highly exalted him and bestowed on him the name that is above every name.' Hyperypsōsen (super-exalted) is a hapax-legomenon in the NT — the hyper prefix intensifies beyond normal exaltation. The 'name above every name' that is then used in v. 10 is YHWH (Kyrios): by giving Jesus the name Lord-of-all, God applies Isa 45:23 (every knee bows to YHWH) to Jesus. The highest OT claim — universal homage to YHWH alone — is now predicated of the crucified and exalted Jesus.
11: kai pasa glossa exomologesetai hoti Kyrios Iesous Christos eis doxan Theou patros (kai pāsa glōssa exomologēsetai hoti Kyrios Iēsous Christos eis doxan Theou patros): 'every tongue confess that Jesus Christ is Lord, to the glory of God the Father.' The confession Kyrios Iesous Christos is the earliest Christian creed (cf. Rom 10:9; 1 Cor 12:3). Its application of Isa 45:23 to Jesus is the most explicit equation of Jesus with YHWH in the undisputed Pauline letters. The eis doxan Theou patros (to the glory of God the Father) prevents the Christology from displacing the Father: Jesus's lordship is the Father's glory, not a rival to it — a proto-Trinitarian formulation.
1: Ei tis oun paraklēsis en Christō, ei ti paramythion agapēs, ei tis koinōnia pneumatos, ei tis splagchna kai oiktirmoi: 'If there is any encouragement (paraklēsis) in Christ, any comfort (paramythion) from love, any participation (koinōnia) in the Spirit, any affection and sympathy (splagchna kai oiktirmoi).' The fourfold conditional assumes all four conditions are real — the Greek idiom treats them as certainly true. Paraklēsis (encouragement, consolation) shares its root with the Paraclete; paramythion (comfort, consolation through words); koinōnia pneumatos (fellowship/sharing of the Spirit). The appeal is to their shared experience of Christ.
2: plērōsate mou tēn charan hina to auto phronēte tēn autēn agapēn echontes sympsychoi to hen phronountes: 'complete my joy by being of the same mind (to auto phronēte), having the same love, being in full accord (sympsychoi — one-souled) and of one mind (to hen phronountes).' The double use of phronein (to have the same mindset) frames the unity appeal. Sympsychoi (knit together in soul) — a Pauline hapax. The unity Paul seeks is not merely behavioral conformity but deep shared orientation and affection.
3: mēden kat' eritheian mēde kata kenodoxian alla tē tapeinophrosynē allēlous hēgoumenoi hyperechontas heautōn: 'Do nothing from selfish ambition (eritheian) or conceit (kenodoxian, empty glory/vainglory), but in humility (tapeinophrosynē) count others more significant than yourselves.' Tapeinophrosynē (humility, lowliness of mind) was not a virtue in Greco-Roman culture — it was associated with the servile, the defeated, the socially inferior. Paul installs it as the Christian alternative to Roman honor-competition. Hēgoumenoi hyperechontas heautōn: reckoning others as surpassing oneself — a radical counter-cultural posture.
4: mē ta heautōn hekastos skopeite alla [kai] ta heterōn hekastos: 'Let each of you look not only to his own interests, but also to the interests of others.' Skopeite (observe, look to, attend to) — the active consideration of others' concerns alongside one's own. This verse provides the ethical frame that the Christ-hymn (vv.6-11) then grounds christologically: the pattern of not-grasping-for-oneself is the Christ-pattern.
5: touto phroneite en hymin ho kai en Christō Iēsou: 'Have this mind (touto phroneite) among yourselves, which is yours in Christ Jesus.' The transition to the Christ-hymn: the mindset Paul calls for is already present in the community through Christ. Phronein here introduces the Christological basis for the ethical appeal. The 'mind of Christ' is not an abstract ideal but the attitude concretely embodied in the narrative of vv.6-11.
7: alla heauton ekenōsen morphēn doulou labōn en homoiōmati anthrōpōn genomenos: 'but emptied himself (ekenōsen), by taking the form of a servant (morphēn doulou labōn), being born in the likeness (homoiōmati) of men.' Ekenōsen (from kenoō) — to empty, make void. The Kenotic Christology centers on this verb: the divine Son voluntarily relinquished (not his divine nature but) the independent exercise of divine prerogatives, taking instead the form of a slave. Morphēn doulou: the form of a doulos (slave/servant) is the direct antithesis of morphē theou. The Son of God took the social position of the lowest: the slave who has no rights, whose entire existence is defined by another's will.
8: kai schēmati heuretheis hōs anthrōpos etapeinōsen heauton genomenos hypēkoos mechri thanatou, thanatou de staurou: 'And being found in human form (schēmati), he humbled himself by becoming obedient to the point of death, even death on a cross.' Schēma (form, appearance, external configuration — as in the schematic of a plan): Christ was found in the outward configuration of a human being. Etapeinōsen heauton (he humbled himself) — active self-humiliation. Mechri thanatou, thanatou de staurou: 'even to death — death on a cross.' The shocking climax: the cross was Rome's most shameful execution, reserved for the lowest criminals and slaves. The Son of God died a slave's death.
10: hina en tō onomati Iēsou pan gony kampsē epouraniōn kai epigeiōn kai katachthoniōn: 'so that at the name of Jesus every knee should bow, in heaven and on earth and under the earth.' The three-level cosmos (heavenly/earthly/subterranean — the classic ancient cosmological division: gods/humans/the dead) bows before the name of Jesus. Kampsē (bend, bow down) — the physical act of submission. The citation of Isa 45:23 applies the absolute claim of YHWH's universal sovereignty to the exalted Jesus.
12: hōste agapētoi mou kathōs pantote hypēkousate mē hōs en tē parousia mou monon alla nyn pollō mallon en tē apousia mou, meta phobou kai tromou tēn heautōn sōtērian katergazesthe: 'Therefore, my beloved, as you have always obeyed, so now, not only as in my presence (parousia) but much more in my absence (apousia), work out your own salvation (sōtērian katergazesthe) with fear and trembling (phobou kai tromou).' Katergazesthe (work out, accomplish, bring to completion) — not earn but work out the implications of received salvation. Phobos kai tromos: OT language for the awe of divine encounter (Exod 15:16; Ps 2:11). The fear is reverence before the God who is at work (v.13), not fear of loss of salvation.
13: ho theos gar estin ho energōn en hymin kai to thelein kai to energein hyper tēs eudokias: 'for it is God who works (ho energōn) in you, both to will (to thelein) and to work (to energein) for his good pleasure (eudokias).' The divine-human cooperation: God works in them (the indicative ground) so they work out their salvation (the imperative response). Even the will to obey is God's gift. Eudokia (good pleasure, benevolent purpose) — God's working has its source in his own gracious purpose, not in the congregation's merit.
14: panta poieite chōris gongysmōn kai dialogismōn: 'Do all things without grumbling (gongysmōn) or disputing (dialogismōn).' Gongysmoi (murmurings, grumblings) — the LXX word for Israel's wilderness complaints against Moses and God (Exod 16:7-12; Num 11:1; 14:27). The echo is deliberate: the Philippians are to do the opposite of Israel in the wilderness. Dialogismoi (disputes, questionings, debates) — internal community conflict that undermines the unified mission.
15: hina genēsthe amemptoi kai akeraioi tekna theou amōma mesē geneas skolias kai diestrammenēs en hois phainesthē hōs phōstēres en kosmō: 'that you may be blameless (amemptoi) and innocent (akeraioi), children of God without blemish (amōma) in the midst of a crooked and twisted generation, among whom you shine as lights (phōstēres) in the world.' Amōmos (without blemish — the sacrificial standard) combined with phōstēres (lights, luminaries — the stars of Dan 12:3: those who are wise shall shine like the brightness of the sky). The congregation is Israel-in-the-wilderness reconstituted as the light-bearing community in the crooked generation.
16: logon zōēs epechontes eis kauchēma emoi eis hēmeran Christou, hoti ouk eis kenon edramon oude eis kenon ekopiasa: 'holding fast (epechontes) to the word of life, so that in the day of Christ I may be proud that I did not run in vain (eis kenon edramon) or labor in vain.' The athletic metaphor: edramon (I ran) — Paul's apostolic work as a race (1 Cor 9:24-26; Gal 2:2). The fear is kenon (empty, vain) — that the Philippians' failure would nullify the apostolic effort at the day of Christ.
17: alla ei kai spendomai epi tē thysia kai leitourgia tēs pisteōs hymōn chariō kai synchairō pasin hymin: 'Even if I am to be poured out as a drink offering (spendomai) upon the sacrificial offering and service (leitourgia) of your faith, I am glad and rejoice with you all.' Spendomai (I am poured out as a libation) — the Roman and Jewish sacrificial practice of pouring wine on the altar as a drink offering. Paul's potential martyrdom is reframed as a libation poured over the Philippians' faith as the primary sacrifice. The apostle's death would be an additional offering over their living faith-offering.
18: to de auto kai hymeis chairete kai synchairete moi: 'Likewise you also should be glad and rejoice with me.' The mutual joy: even if Paul is executed, both he and the Philippians should rejoice — because his death would be a sacrificial act completing the gospel work. Joy is not mood-dependent but stems from the theological reality of what suffering means in Christ.
19: elpizō de en kyriō Iēsou Timotheon tacheōs pempsai hymin hina kagō euthymō gnous ta peri hymōn: 'I hope in the Lord Jesus to send Timothy to you soon, so that I too may be cheered by news of you.' The hope is qualified en kyriō Iēsou (in the Lord Jesus) — Paul's plans are subject to the Lord's will (cf. Jas 4:15). Timothy as emissary: his sending extends Paul's own pastoral concern.
20: oudena gar echo isopsychon hostis gnēsiōs ta peri hymōn merimnēsei: 'For I have no one like him (isopsychon — of equal soul), who will be genuinely concerned for your welfare.' Isopsychos (equal-souled, like-minded) — a Pauline hapax. Timothy's genuine (gnēsiōs — legitimately, not pretended) care for the Philippians mirrors Paul's own. The others seek their own interests (v.21), not the things of Christ Jesus — an implicit contrast with the Christ-hymn's self-emptying pattern.
21: hoi pantes gar ta heautōn zētousin, ou ta Iesou Christou: 'For they all seek their own interests, not those of Jesus Christ.' The stark contrast to the Christ-hymn's principle (v.4: look not to your own interests) and to Timothy's character. Those seeking their own interests represent the worldly pattern; Timothy represents the Christomorphic pattern.
22: tēn de dokimēn autou ginōskete hoti hōs patri teknon syn emoi edouleusen eis to euangelion: 'But you know Timothy's proven worth (dokimē), how as a son with a father he has served with me in the gospel.' Dokimē (proven character, tested quality — from dokimazō: to test by fire). The father-son relationship describes the apprenticeship dynamic of apostolic formation: Timothy learned gospel ministry by serving alongside Paul.
23: touton men oun elpizō pempsai hōs an aphidō ta peri eme exautēs: 'I hope therefore to send him just as soon as I see how it will go with me.' The uncertainty about Paul's legal outcome governs the timing. He cannot leave himself until the situation is resolved; Timothy is his advance ambassador.
24: pepoitha de en kyriō hoti kai autos tacheōs eleusomai: 'and I trust in the Lord that shortly I myself will come also.' The personal visit anticipated en kyriō — subject to divine permission. Despite imprisonment, Paul anticipates release and reunion with the congregation he loves.
25: anagkaion de hēgēsamēn Epaphroditon ton adelphon kai synergon kai systratiōtēn mou hymōn de apostolon kai leitourgon tēs chreias mou pempsai pros hymas: 'I have thought it necessary to send to you Epaphroditus my brother and fellow worker (synergon) and fellow soldier (systratiōtēn), and your messenger (apostolon) and minister (leitourgon) to my need.' The five-fold description of Epaphroditus: brother, co-worker, co-soldier (evoking military partnership), apostolos (sent one — the Philippians' envoy, using the same term as Paul's apostleship), leitourgos (priestly minister — a cultic term for official service). The priestly vocabulary elevates his service into an act of worship.
26: epeidē epipothōn ēn pantas hymas kai adēmonōn dioti ēkousate hoti ēsthenēsen: 'for he has been longing for you all and has been distressed because you heard that he was ill.' Epipothōn (longing for) — the same verb used for Paul's longing for the Philippians (1:8). Adēmonōn (distressed, troubled in spirit) — used in Mark 14:33 for Jesus in Gethsemane. Epaphroditus's pastoral concern for the congregation's anxiety about him is itself a Christomorphic trait.
27: kai gar ēsthenēsen paraplēsion thanatō alla ho theos ēleēsen auton, ouk auton de monon alla kai eme hina mē lypēn epi lypēn schō: 'Indeed he was ill, near to death. But God had mercy on him, and not only on him but on me also, lest I should have sorrow upon sorrow.' The honest acknowledgment of Epaphroditus's near-fatal illness — prayer does not always prevent suffering, but God showed mercy. The lypēn epi lypēn (sorrow upon sorrow) Paul would have suffered: his own imprisonment-sorrow plus the grief of losing his co-worker.
28: spoudaioterōs oun epempsa auton hina idontes auton palin charēte kagō alypoteros ō: 'I am the more eager to send him, therefore, that you may rejoice at seeing him again, and that I may be less anxious.' The mutual joy of reunion: the congregation's rejoicing at Epaphroditus's return becomes Paul's relief. The pastoral economy of shared joy circulates throughout the letter.
29: prsdechesthe oun auton en kyriō meta pasēs charas kai tous toioutous entimous echete: 'So receive him in the Lord with all joy (meta pasēs charas), and honor (entimous) such men.' The community is to honor those who have served to the point of death (v.30). Entimos (honored, precious, valued) — the counter-cultural reversal: Paul calls the congregation to honor the servant-martyr rather than the socially powerful.
30: hoti dia to ergon Christou mechri thanatou ēngisen paraboleusamenos tē psychē hina anaplērōsē to hymōn hysterēma tēs pros me leitourgias: 'for he nearly died for the work of Christ, risking his life (paraboleusamenos tē psychē) to complete what was lacking (hysterēma) in your service to me.' Paraboleusamenos (risking, gambling with — from parabolos, one who ventures, takes risks): Epaphroditus gambled his life in service. The hysterēma (lack, deficiency) the Philippians could not supply in person because of distance — Epaphroditus supplied it by his presence and service.
